content.text: GenAI applications are PII magnets. Users ask questions about their health, finances, relationships, and business operations. Even when the application instructs users not to enter personal information, they do. Prompt text that makes it into logs creates a
that conventional web applications never face at the same scale.
PII scrubbing in log ingestion pipelines is a

with four layers: prevention at the source, redaction at the collector, schema enforcement at the ingest gateway, and nightly audit scanning to catch what slipped through. No single layer is sufficient; all four must work together.

attrs.alt: Four-layer PII scrubbing architecture: source → collector → gateway → audit scanner

content.text: What Counts as PII in GenAI Logs
The PII surface in GenAI logs is broader than in conventional APIs:
Where it appears in GenAI logs
User questions, error context, metadata fields
User prompts about purchases or finances
User prompts about tax or benefits
User prompts asking for help with code
The cheapest place to prevent PII in logs is at the code that writes the log. A
processor runs on every log call before any handler serialises the record. The processor redacts known-sensitive field names and applies regex patterns to field values.

Performance note: regex compilation is expensive. Compile all patterns at module load time (as shown above) rather than inside the processor function. A processor that runs on every log call must be fast — budget < 0.1 ms per call.

Even with source-level scrubbing, some logs will contain PII — from third-party libraries, framework error handlers, or exception messages that capture request context. The log collector (Fluentd, Vector, or Promtail) is the

# fluent.conf — PII scrubbing filter
<filter genai.**>
  @type record_transformer
  enable_ruby true
  <record>
    # Redact known field names
    user_email    ${record['user_email'] ? '[REDACTED]' : nil}
    raw_prompt    ${record['raw_prompt'] ? '[REDACTED]' : nil}
  </record>
  remove_keys password,api_key,credit_card
</filter>

<filter genai.**>
  @type grep
  # Drop lines whose message contains likely prompt content
  <exclude>
    key message
    pattern /raw_prompt|completion_text/i
  </exclude>
</filter>
Loki and OpenSearch both support field-level deny-listing at ingestion time. This layer catches anything that survived layers 1 and 2.

# promtail/config.yaml — scrub at label extraction stage
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: genai-completion
    pipeline_stages:
      - json:
          expressions:
            event: event
            trace_id: trace_id
            level: level
            model: model
            feature_name: feature_name
            # Intentionally NOT extracting: user_email, raw_prompt, completion_text
            # These fields are simply ignored at parse time

      - drop:
          # Drop log lines where the 'event' field is empty (malformed)
          expression: '{{ if not .event }}true{{ end }}'

      - replace:
          # Regex scrub on the raw log line before Loki stores it
          expression: '(\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}\b)'
          replace: '[EMAIL]'

      - replace:
          expression: '(\b[0-9]{3}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{4}\b)'
          replace: '[SSN]'

      - replace:
          expression: '(sk-[A-Za-z0-9]{32,})'
          replace: '[API_KEY]'

      - labels:
          event:
          level:
          model:
          feature_name:
— it scans stored logs daily to find PII that slipped through, fires an alert, and triggers auto-deletion of the affected log stream.
The audit job runs as a Kubernetes
every night at 02:00 UTC. It never stores the matched log lines — only hashes and pattern labels — to avoid the scanner itself becoming a PII store.

— a record of what users submitted — for safety review, abuse detection, or regulatory requirements. These logs are fundamentally different from operational logs and must be treated as a separate system:
separate Loki tenant or Elasticsearch index
— not accessible via the operational log Grafana instance
) — not inherited from general observability access
Every query to the prompt audit store is itself
logged as an access audit event
Retention period is contractually defined (often 30–365 days per industry)
collected at the product level — not a default-on logging practice

# Separate Vector sink for prompt audit logs
[sinks.prompt_audit]
type = "loki"
inputs = ["prompt_audit_transform"]   # separate pipeline, not the main genai pipeline
endpoint = "https://loki-audit.internal.example.com"   # separate Loki instance
auth.strategy = "bearer"
auth.token = "${LOKI_AUDIT_TOKEN}"
labels.tenant = "prompt-audit"
labels.env = "{{ env }}"
encoding.codec = "json"

# Only receives events from the explicit audit logging path, not default log flow
All four layers need automated tests. The most important test: inject known PII into the pipeline and assert it does not appear in the stored logs.
Even with scrubbing, logs contain sensitive metadata — which users are using which features, error patterns, cost data. Access control is a non-negotiable complement to scrubbing:
prod logs for their team's services, read-only
staging + dev logs for their feature
Eval pipeline logs, quality metrics only
Aggregated cost metrics only (no raw logs)
In Grafana, implement access control via
— each team's Loki datasource has its own connection that includes a
header restricting which tenant streams they can query. Engineers cannot cross tenant boundaries even if they craft a raw Loki API query.
: identify the affected log stream and time range
: delete the affected stream in Loki using
: trace back to the source — which service version logged the PII, which processor failed to scrub it
: fix the source and deploy a patch; re-run the audit scanner to confirm clean
: notify the data protection officer if the PII type is GDPR-notifiable (email, health data, financial data) and assess if notification to affected users is required

# Delete a specific log stream from Loki (irreversible)
# Use only after confirming the stream selector with read queries first
LOKI_URL="https://loki.example.com"
TOKEN="..."

# First: confirm the stream exists and contains PII
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "$LOKI_URL/loki/api/v1/query_range" \
  --data-urlencode 'query={service="genai-completion", env="prod", level="debug"}' \
  --data-urlencode "limit=5" | jq '.data.result[].values[0][1]'

# Delete the stream
curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "$LOKI_URL/loki/api/v1/delete" \
  --data-urlencode 'query={service="genai-completion", env="prod", level="debug"}' \
  --data-urlencode "start=$(date -d '2 days ago' +%s)000000000" \
  --data-urlencode "end=$(date +%s)000000000"
PII scrubbing in GenAI log pipelines requires defense-in-depth across four layers because no single layer catches everything reliably.
Layer 1 (source): structlog processor redacts forbidden fields and applies PII regex before any handler serialises the record
Layer 2 (collector): Fluentd/Vector/Promtail applies JSON field scrubbing and regex redaction on the full log line as a safety net
Layer 3 (gateway): Loki pipeline_stages ignore known-sensitive field names and apply regex replacement at parse time
Layer 4 (audit): Nightly CronJob scans stored logs with extended patterns, fires a critical alert on any match, and triggers stream deletion
Prompt audit logs are a separate system with explicit consent, restricted RBAC, access auditing, and contractual retention
PII leak incidents follow a 15/30/60/120-minute response timeline with mandatory DPO notification for GDPR-notifiable data types
